name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ قَدْ جَآءَكُمُ ٱلرَّسُولُ بِٱلْحَقِّ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ خَيْرًا لَّكُمْ ۚ وَإِن تَكْفُرُوا۟ فَإِنَّ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (١٧٠)
```

```english
O mankind! The Apostle has certainly brought you the truth from your Lord. So have faith! That is better for you. And if you are faithless, [you should know that] to Allah indeed belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (170)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَا تَغْلُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْحَقَّ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ وَكَلِمَتُهُۥٓ أَلْقَىٰهَآ إِلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ وَرُوحٌ مِّنْهُ ۖ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ ۖ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ ثَلَـٰثَةٌ ۚ ٱنتَهُوا۟ خَيْرًا لَّكُمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱللَّهُ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۖ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥٓ أَن يَكُونَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌ ۘ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًا (١٧١)
```

```english
O People of the Book! Do not exceed the bounds in your religion, and do not attribute anything to Allah except the truth. The Messiah, Jesus son of Mary, was only an apostle of Allah, and His Word that He cast toward Mary and a spirit from Him. So have faith in Allah and His apostles, and do not say, ‘[God is] a trinity.’ Relinquish [such a creed]! That is better for you. Allah is but the One God. He is far too immaculate to have any son. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and Allah suffices as trustee. (171)
```

```arabic
لَّن يَسْتَنكِفَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ أَن يَكُونَ عَبْدًا لِّلَّهِ وَلَا ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ ٱلْمُقَرَّبُونَ ۚ وَمَن يَسْتَنكِفْ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِۦ وَيَسْتَكْبِرْ فَسَيَحْشُرُهُمْ إِلَيْهِ جَمِيعًا (١٧٢)
```

```english
The Messiah would never disdain being a servant of Allah, nor would the angels brought near [to Him]. And whoever disdains His worship and is arrogant, He will gather them all toward Him. (172)
```

```arabic
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَيُوَفِّيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ وَيَزِيدُهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦ ۖ وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَنكَفُوا۟ وَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ فَيُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا وَلَا يَجِدُونَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا (١٧٣)
```

```english
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, He will pay them in full their rewards, and He will enhance them out of His grace. But those who are disdainful and arrogant, He will punish them with a painful punishment, and they will not find besides Allah any guardian or helper. (173)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ قَدْ جَآءَكُم بُرْهَـٰنٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَأَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكُمْ نُورًا مُّبِينًا (١٧٤)
```

```english
O mankind! Certainly, a proof has come to you from your Lord, and We have sent down to you a manifest light. (174)
```

```arabic
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱعْتَصَمُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَسَيُدْخِلُهُمْ فِى رَحْمَةٍ مِّنْهُ وَفَضْلٍ وَيَهْدِيهِمْ إِلَيْهِ صِرَٰطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا (١٧٥)
```

```english
As for those who have faith in Allah, and hold fast to Him, He will admit them to His mercy and grace, and He will guide them on a straight path to Him. (175)
```

## (Commentary)

After responding to the proposal of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) regarding their request that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله bring down a book from heaven—by clarifying that His Messenger has indeed come with the truth from his Lord, and that the Book he brought from his Lord is a definitive proof (حجة قاطعة) in which there is no doubt—He concluded from this the validity of inviting all people to His Prophet and His Book.

He had previously explained that all His messengers and prophets—among whom He mentioned Jesus—follow a single tradition (سنة) with identical parts and aspects, which is the tradition of revelation (الوحي) from Allah. From this, He concluded the validity of inviting the Christians—who are people of a Book and revelation—not to exceed the bounds (لا يغلوا) in their religion, to join the rest of the monotheists (الموحدين) among the believers, and to affirm regarding Jesus what they and others affirm regarding all other prophets: that they are servants of Allah and His messengers to His creation.

Thus, the Exalted began calling all people to believe in His Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, because what was first clarified was the truth of his prophethood in His saying: **إِنَّا أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ كَما أَوْحَيْنا إِلى نُوحٍ وَالنَّبِيِّينَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ** "We have indeed revealed to you as We revealed to Noah and the prophets after him" (4:163).

Then He called for the avoidance of extremism (الغلو) regarding the right of Jesus عليه‌السلام, as this was the second matter clarified within the aforementioned verses. Then He called for the following of His Book, which is the Noble Qur’an, because it was the final matter clarified in the saying of the Exalted: **لكِنِ اللهُ يَشْهَدُ بِما أَنْزَلَ إِلَيْكَ أَنْزَلَهُ بِعِلْمِهِ** "But Allah bears witness to what He has sent down to you—He sent it down with His knowledge" (4:166).

His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ قَدْ جاءَكُمُ الرَّسُولُ بِالْحَقِّ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ فَآمِنُوا خَيْراً لَكُمْ** "O mankind! The Apostle has certainly brought you the truth from your Lord. So believe; that is better for you" (4:170), is a general address to the People of the Book and all other people. It branches out from the preceding clarification directed at the People of the Book. The address was made general because the object of the invitation—which is belief in the Messenger—is likewise general due to the universality of the message.

His saying, **خَيْراً لَكُمْ** "better for you," is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for "belief," and it is an inseparable circumstance (حال لازمة); meaning, in a state where belief has the inseparable attribute of being better for you.

His saying, **وَإِنْ تَكْفُرُوا فَإِنَّ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** "And if you disbelieve, then to Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth" (4:170), means that if you disbelieve, your disbelief will not add anything to your own detriment, nor will it diminish anything from Allah, the Glorified. For everything in the heavens and the earth belongs to Allah; thus, it is impossible for anything of His kingdom (ملك) to be stripped from Him, the Exalted. Indeed, it is in the very nature (طباع) of everything in the heavens and the earth that it belongs to Allah, without partner. Therefore, its being existent and its being owned are one and the same identity (العينية). How then can something be removed from His kingdom, the Exalted, while it is a "thing" (شيء)?

This verse is among the comprehensive words (الكلمات الجامعة) which, the more one contemplates (تدبر) them, the more they yield an increase in subtlety of meaning and a wondrous breadth in clarification. For the encompassment of His kingdom, the Exalted, over all things and their effects, provides subtle meanings regarding disbelief (الكفر), belief (الإيمان), obedience (الطاعة), and disobedience (المعصية); so you must increase your contemplation upon it.

His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ لا تَغْلُوا فِي دِينِكُمْ وَلا تَقُولُوا عَلَى اللهِ إِلَّا الْحَقَّ** "O People of the Book! Do not exceed the bounds in your religion, and do not attribute anything to Allah except the truth" (4:171). The apparent sense of the address, by the context of what is mentioned therein regarding the matter of the Messiah عليه‌السلام, is that it is an address to the Christians. They were addressed as "People of the Book" (أهل الكتاب)—which is a shared description—to signal that their being named "People of the Book" necessitates that they do not transgress the limits of what Allah has sent down and clarified in His books. Among what He clarified is that they should not say anything about Him except the truth.

It is possible that it is an address to both the Jews and the Christians, for the Jews are also like the Christians in their extremism (غلو) in religion and their saying regarding Allah that which is not the truth, as the Exalted said: **وَقالَتِ الْيَهُودُ عُزَيْرٌ ابْنُ اللهِ** "The Jews say, ‘Ezra is the son of Allah’" (9:30), and the Exalted said: **اتَّخَذُوا أَحْبارَهُمْ وَرُهْبانَهُمْ أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ** "They have taken their scribes and their monks as lords besides Allah" (9:31), and the Exalted said: **قُلْ يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ تَعالَوْا إِلى كَلِمَةٍ سَواءٍ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَكُمْ** "Say, ‘O People of the Book! Come to a word common between us and you...’" until He said: **وَلا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنا بَعْضاً أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ** "...and that none of us take others for lords besides Allah" (3:64).

Based on this, His saying: **إِنَّمَا الْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولُ اللهِ** "The Messiah, Jesus son of Mary, was only an apostle of Allah..." (4:171) is a specification (تخصيص) in the address after the generalization, by addressing a specific group among the addressees with what pertains to them.

However, this is rendered unlikely by the fact that the apparent context suggests that His saying, "The Messiah, Jesus son of Mary, was only an apostle of Allah," serves as the justification (تعليل) for His saying, "Do not exceed the bounds in your religion." This necessitates that the address is specific to the Christians. His saying, **إِنَّمَا الْمَسِيحُ** "The Messiah"—meaning the Blessed—**عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ** "Jesus son of Mary," is an explicit mention of the name and the mother's name so as to be furthest from interpretation (التفسير) or metaphorical construction (التأويل) with any divergent meaning, and to serve as evidence of his being a created human being like any human possessing a mother. **وَكَلِمَتُهُ أَلْقاها إِلى مَرْيَمَ** "and His Word that He cast toward Mary" (4:171) is an explanation of the meaning of "the Word" (الكلمة); for he is the word "Be" (كن) which was cast toward the virgin Mary. Ordinary causes (الأسباب العادية), such as marriage and a father, did not operate in his formation. The Exalted said: **إِذا قَضى أَمْراً فَإِنَّما يَقُولُ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ** "When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is" (3:47). Thus, every thing is a "word" (كلمة) of His, the Exalted, except that all other things are intertwined with ordinary causes. The reason Jesus عليه‌السلام was specifically characterized by the application of the name "the Word" is his lack of some ordinary causes in his birth. **وَرُوحٌ مِنْهُ** "and a spirit from Him" (4:171); and the spirit (الروح) is from the Command (الأمر). The Exalted said: **قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي** "Say, ‘The spirit is of the command of my Lord’" (17:85). Since Jesus عليه‌السلام was the existential word "Be"—which is a Command—he is therefore a spirit.

The discussion regarding this verse has already preceded in the discourse on the creation of the Messiah in the third volume of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَآمِنُوا بِاللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَلا تَقُولُوا ثَلاثَةٌ انْتَهُوا خَيْراً لَكُمْ إِنَّمَا اللهُ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ** "So possess faith in Allah and His apostles, and do not say, ‘[They are] three.’ Abandon [this], it will be better for you. Allah is only one God" (4:171). This is a derivation (تفريع) from the opening of the discourse, as it is justified by His saying, "The Messiah..." etc. Meaning: if the matter is such, then belief (الإيمان) in this manner is obligatory upon you—which is to believe in Allah in His Lordship (الربوبية) and in His messengers—including Jesus—in their messengership (الرسالة). "And do not say, ‘Three.’ Abandon [this]," in a state where this abandonment, or the state where belief in Allah and His messengers and the negation of the three, is better for you.

The "three" (الثلاثة) refers to the three persons (الأقانيم): the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. The discussion on this has already preceded in the verses revealed concerning the matter of the Messiah عليه‌السلام in Surah Al-Imran.

His saying, the Exalted: **سُبْحانَهُ أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ وَلَدٌ لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ** "Immaculate is He from having any offspring. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth" (4:171). The word "Immaculate" (سبحان) is an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) with an implied verb, to which His saying **أَنْ يَكُونَ** "from having" relates; it is in the accusative case due to the removal of the preposition (نزع الخافض). The estimation is: "I glorify Him with a glorification (أسبحه تسبيحا) and I declare Him transcendent with a transcendence (أنزهه تنزيها) from having a child." The sentence is a parenthetical insertion (جملة اعتراض) brought for the purpose of glorification (التعظيم).

His saying, **لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ** "To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth," is either a circumstantial qualifier (حال) or an introductory sentence (جملة استيناف). In either case, it is an argumentation (احتجاج) for the negation of offspring from Him, the Glorified. For a child, however conceived, is that which resembles the progenitor in the core of its essence (سنخ ذاته), being formed from it. Since everything in the heavens and the earth is owned in the origin of its essence and its effects by Allah the Exalted—and He alone is the All-Sustainer (القيوم) — *the Self-Subsisting One who maintains and oversees all existence* — of every thing, then nothing among these things resembles Him; thus, He has no child.

The context is one of generalization for everything in existence other than Allah, exalted is His Name. The consequence of this is that His saying, "whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth," is a metonymic expression (تعبير كنائي) for everything besides Allah, the Glorified, since the heavens and the earth themselves are included in this proof, and they are not merely "within" the heavens and the earth, but are the heavens and the earth themselves.

Then, since the command and prohibition in the verse constitute a general guidance for them toward what is better for them in their worldly life and the Hereafter, He concluded the speech with His saying: **وَكَفى بِاللهِ وَكِيلاً** "and Allah suffices as a guardian" (4:171); meaning, as a guardian (ولي) of your affairs and a disposer (مدبر) of your matters, who guides you to what is better for you and calls you to a straight path (صراط مستقيم).

His saying, the Exalted: **لَنْ يَسْتَنْكِفَ الْمَسِيحُ أَنْ يَكُونَ عَبْداً لِلَّهِ وَلَا الْمَلائِكَةُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ** "The Messiah would never disdain to be a servant of Allah, nor would the angels brought near [to Him]" (4:172). This is another argumentation (احتجاج) for the negation of the divinity of the Messiah عليه‌السلام absolutely, whether he is assumed to be a son or the third of three. For the Messiah is a servant of Allah who would never disdain His worship (العبادة); this is something the Christians do not deny. The Gospels circulating among them are explicit that he used to worship Allah the Exalted. There is no meaning to the worship of a "son" who is of the same divine essence, nor to the worship of a thing for itself, nor to the worship of one of the three for the third, whose existence applies to each of them. This proof has already been discussed in the sections concerning the Messiah عليه‌السلام.

His saying, **وَلَا الْمَلائِكَةُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ** "nor would the angels brought near," is a generalization of the discourse to include the angels, because the same argument applies to them. A group of the polytheists—such as the polytheists of the Arabs—claimed they were the daughters of Allah; thus, the phrase is digressive (استطرادية).

The expression in the verse—namely His saying, "The Messiah would never disdain to be a servant of Allah, nor would the angels brought near"—referring to Jesus عليه‌السلام as "the Messiah" and describing the angels as "brought near" (المقربين), signals causality (العلية) due to the meaning inherent in these descriptions. That is, Jesus would never disdain His worship—and how could he disdain it when he is a blessed Messiah? Nor would the angels, while they are brought near? If it were expected of them to disdain, Allah would not have blessed the former nor brought the latter near. Allah has also described the Messiah as being "brought near" in His saying: **وَجِيهاً فِي الدُّنْيا وَالْآخِرَةِ وَمِنَ الْمُقَرَّبِينَ** "honoured in the world and the Hereafter, and one of those brought near [to Allah]" (3:45).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَسْتَنْكِفْ عَنْ عِبادَتِهِ وَيَسْتَكْبِرْ فَسَيَحْشُرُهُمْ إِلَيْهِ جَمِيعاً** "And whoever disdains His worship and is arrogant, He will gather them all toward Him" (4:172). This is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the Messiah and the angels, and it stands in the place of justification (تعليل). Meaning: how could the Messiah and the angels brought near disdain His worship, while the situation is that those among His servants—from humans, jinn, and angels—who disdain His worship and are arrogant will all be gathered toward Him, to be recompensed according to their deeds? The Messiah and the angels know this, believe in it, and fear Him.

Among the evidence that His saying, "And whoever disdains His worship and is arrogant..." etc., means that the Messiah and the angels brought near are aware that the disdainful will be gathered toward Him, is His saying, **وَيَسْتَكْبِرْ** "and is arrogant." He only qualified "whoever disdains" with this because mere disdain (الاستنكاف) does not necessitate divine wrath if it is not out of arrogance (استكبار), as is the case with the ignorant and the oppressed (المستضعفين). As for the Messiah and the angels, their disdain could only be out of arrogance because they are aware of the station of their Lord. Therefore, He sufficed with mentioning only "disdain" regarding them. Thus, the meaning of justifying this with His saying, "And whoever disdains His worship and is arrogant," is that they are aware that whoever disdains His worship... etc.

His saying, **جَمِيعاً** "all," means both the righteous and the wicked. This is what validates the differentiation that follows in His saying: **فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ** "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds..." (4:173).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا يَجِدُونَ لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ وَلِيًّا وَلا نَصِيراً** "and they will not find for themselves any guardian or helper besides Allah" (4:173). The mention of the negation of a guardian (ولي) and a helper (نصير) is in opposition to what was claimed regarding the divinity of the Messiah and the angels.

His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ قَدْ جاءَكُمْ بُرْهانٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكُمْ نُوراً مُبِيناً** "O mankind! Certainly a proof has come to you from your Lord, and We have sent down to you a manifest light" (4:174). Al-Raghib said: "The proof (البرهان) is the clarification of the argument (الحجة); it is on the pattern of *fu'lan* like *rujhan* and *thunyan*." Others said: "It is a verbal noun from *baraha yabruhu* when it becomes white." In any case, it is a verbal noun (مصدر). It is sometimes used in the sense of the active participle (الفاعل), as when it is applied to the evidence and the argument itself.

What is meant by "the light" (النور) is undoubtedly the Qur’an, by the context of His saying, **وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكُمْ** "and We have sent down to you." It is possible that "the proof" (البرهان) also refers to it, in which case the two sentences emphasize one another.

It is also possible that it refers to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. This is supported by the occurrence of the verse at the conclusion of the verses clarifying the truth of the Prophet in his message and the descent of the Qur’an from Allah, and by the fact that the verse is a derivation from that. It is also supported by the saying of the Exalted in the following verse, **وَاعْتَصَمُوا بِهِ** "and hold fast to Him" (4:175), because it was previously mentioned in the discourse on His saying, **وَمَنْ يَعْتَصِمْ بِاللهِ فَقَدْ هُدِيَ إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ** "And whoever holds fast to Allah is certainly guided to a straight path" (3:101), that "holding fast" (الاعتصام) means taking up the Book of Allah and following His Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللهِ وَاعْتَصَمُوا بِهِ** "As for those who have faith in Allah, and hold fast to Him..." (4:175), is a clarification of the reward for those who follow the proof of their Lord and the light sent down from Him.

The verse is as if extracted from the previous verse clarifying the reward of those who believe and do righteous deeds—namely His saying: **فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ فَيُوَفِّيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ وَيَزِيدُهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ** "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, He will pay them their rewards in full, and He will enhance them out of His grace" (4:173). Perhaps for this reason, the recompense for those who fail to follow the proof and the light is not mentioned here, because it is identical to what was mentioned in the previous verse. Thus, there is no need to repeat it after signaling that the recompense of the followers here is the recompense of the followers there; and there are only two groups: the followers and those who fail.

Based on this, His saying in this verse, **فَسَيُدْخِلُهُمْ فِي رَحْمَةٍ مِنْهُ** "He will admit them into His mercy," corresponds to His saying in that verse, **فَيُوَفِّيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ** "He will pay them their rewards in full," which is Paradise. Also, His saying in this verse, **وَفَضْلٍ** "and grace," corresponds to His saying in that verse, **وَيَزِيدُهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ** "and He will enhance them out of His grace." As for His saying, **وَيَهْدِيهِمْ إِلَيْهِ صِراطاً مُسْتَقِيماً** "and He will guide them to Himself by a straight path," it is among the effects of what was mentioned therein regarding holding fast (الاعتصام) to Allah, as in His saying: **وَمَنْ يَعْتَصِمْ بِاللهِ فَقَدْ هُدِيَ إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ** "And whoever holds fast to Allah is certainly guided to a straight path" (3:101).
